source_title: Exclusive Interview with Professor Jiang by Dr James Cheong 江学勤独家专访 详细分析美国 伊朗 日本 全世界

interactions.summary: James Cheong introduces Jiang at a Hong Kong Hang Seng University lecture, names his education and books, presents him as a geopolitical analyst with recent predictions, and sets the event as a lecture followed by questions from students, faculty, and online attendees.

Jiang frames the lecture as a comparison between Chinese and Western geopolitical outlooks. He says the Western mind is anchored in Thucydides and the Peloponnesian War, while the Chinese mind is anchored in The Art of War and Romance of the Three Kingdoms, then names utility versus character and stratagem versus speech as the first two differences.

Jiang completes the three-part contrast by opposing genius-centered Chinese history to Thucydidean structural force, then returns to utility versus character through Mao, Deng, Athens, Sparta, and the Iran war.

He contrasts Chinese strategem stories with Pericles funeral oration, arguing that Western speeches create common identity and explain why a people fights; he applies this to Iran’s ability to resist despite military inferiority.

Jiang says Khamenei’s refusal of sanctuary and death function as a speech, then moves from Chinese genius archetypes to the Thucydidean ocean-wave model where historical currents overpower individual swimmers.

He presents Mackinder’s Heartland thesis as the deeper cause of the Iran war: Anglo-American naval strategy must prevent a unified Eurasian Heartland, especially Russia-Iran alignment. He then introduces Dugin’s Foundations of Geopolitics and Third Rome as a Russian grand strategy of positioning within world-historical forces.

Jiang applies the lecture to China: the Iran war is terrible for an export-and-resource-dependent China; Taiwan is not the main flashpoint; overfishing and later water from the Tibetan Plateau are bigger strategic problems. He closes the lecture with three lessons about ideas, geography, and grand strategy.
The host opens Q&A by asking whether Iran avoids striking Jerusalem because Israel might use a false-flag destruction of a mosque to trigger religious war.

Jiang answers that Iran is acting strategically by avoiding symbolic low-value targets and instead pressuring the UAE, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, and other GCC states to force a U.S. ground invasion, because Iran can only defeat America on the ground. He provocatively says Israel and Iran are strategically working toward the destruction of American empire.

The host asks about secret societies and a future Israel-Iran deal; Jiang replies that secret societies do not control everything but preserve power during political change by using long historical memory to accelerate events and profit from imperial decline.
The host asks about an alleged Albert Pike plan for World War III, Muslims and Christians destroying each other, and whether the war will end quickly or exhaust both sides.

Jiang answers the Albert Pike question by distinguishing a fake World War III letter from Pike’s real Morals and Dogma. He says the real project is not cartoon world conquest but a rational order that reduces agency, diversity, and conflict through AI surveillance and control.

The host asks whether Gulf elites will side with America; Jiang says Saudi and GCC ruling families are Anglo-American-installed and vulnerable, and Iran’s strategy is to produce internal conflict that overthrows these elites.
The host asks why Jiang emphasizes three Illuminati components; Jiang names Jacob Frank, Mayer Rothschild, and Adam Weishaupt as the key founding figures in his account.

The host asks what influence secret societies will have on future war expansion. Jiang says they preserve power by reading historical patterns, manipulating events, and using religion as a story that motivates sincere followers such as Christian Zionists.

The host asks how Japan will respond if both the Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea/Bab el-Mandeb route are closed. Jiang says Japan is already under energy pressure and will likely remilitarize, join a coalition, and send naval forces to the Middle East after trying to bribe Iran.

The host suggests Japan can use overseas deployment to revise limits on its self-defense posture and asks whether Japanese militarism will clash with China. Jiang answers that China is a land army focused on borders while Japan is an aggressive naval power, and China can do little if Japan uses the Iran war to militarize.

The host asks whether Japan’s life-and-death energy position could quickly create a challenge to China. Jiang agrees Japan wants the Middle East war as an excuse to remilitarize, but adds Japan can buy oil from Russia and America and is not truly under existential threat.
The host turns to the Philippines and North Korea as additional hot spots that may affect China’s future and asks whether war is possible.

Jiang answers the North Korea/Philippines hot-spot question: North Korea should exploit global distraction and its Russia pact to threaten South Korea and Japan for payoffs, while the Philippines conflict would be part of a larger Southeast Asian alignment against China.

The host asks whether China and America can reconcile or must fight economically or militarily. Jiang answers from American grand strategy: America will lose the Middle East, shift from finance/dollar extraction to selling resources and weapons, and benefit from Japan-China and China-Southeast Asia conflicts.

The host asks whether China can replace America or form a G2 order within ten years. Jiang says China historically has not sought world leadership, lacks grand strategy, and will likely turtle up when Pax Americana collapses.

The host asks what grand policy would be best for China if Jiang could design one. Jiang says he cannot answer because China’s leaders do not care about the world, do not have friends, and therefore lack the precondition for grand strategy.

The host asks whether Greater Israel will emerge within two years and whether Iran and GCC states will still exist. Jiang predicts a dragging Middle East war, U.S. ground troops, destroyed GCC economies, unrest in America, Trump refusing to leave office, and eventually Israel absorbing U.S. CENTCOM apparatus as America exits.
The host asks whether U.S. domestic chaos could become severe enough to divide into different states if the war proceeds as Jiang describes.

Jiang answers the U.S. chaos question by saying America is already in a civil war between Wall Street financial elites and Silicon Valley/AI counter-elites. Wars weaken the military-industrial and financial sectors, making space for a counterrevolution, until financial elites strike back and militias fight in the streets.
The host thanks Jiang and the audience, asks people to follow future Hong Kong Hang Seng University lectures, and Jiang thanks him.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is the host introducing Jiang and the event structure; the later English speaker is Jiang.
SPEAKER_00 is Jiang delivering the lecture; the ASR splits names such as Thucydides and Romance of the Three Kingdoms.
Continuous Jiang lecture. The transcript renders Thucydides as several damaged forms, but the referent is clear from context.
Continuous Jiang lecture; Dugin, Mackinder, and Thucydides are ASR-damaged but contextually clear.
SPEAKER_01 is asking a substantive question; this should be eligible for public questions if answered directly.
Segments 0033-0039 are mostly Jiang, but ASR shifts into Chinese and includes damaged names and clauses; the strategic movement is clear while some details are low confidence.
Q&A moves between English answer and Chinese questions; ASR renders terms like crypto Jews, Sabbatean/Frankist, and Illuminati unevenly. Treat as Jiang’s attributed terminology, not normalized canon.
The question about Yemen/Red Sea/Hormuz and Japan is substantive and answered directly by Jiang.
These are substantive host questions about Japan, China, Philippines, and North Korea; Jiang directly answers Japan and the next packet answers North Korea/Philippines.
Questions are performed by the host and answered directly; they should be eligible public questions with concise Jiang answers.
The host asks several explicit questions; Jiang directly answers the China policy and Greater Israel questions, and the civil-war answer continues in the next packet.
SPEAKER_00 is Jiang giving the final answer; SPEAKER_01 closes the event in Chinese.

claims.claim: Jiang defines the Western geopolitical imagination as Thucydidean: states have souls, psychologies, emotional states, and personalities that drive action beyond utility.
He defines the Chinese geopolitical imagination as more utilitarian and stratagem-centered, rooted in Sunzi and Romance of the Three Kingdoms.
Jiang argues that strategems travel poorly onto the international stage because they assume a shared cultural outlook; speeches matter more because they reveal and coordinate national personality.
Jiang says Chinese historical imagination overvalues genius figures such as Zhuge Liang and Sima Yi, while Thucydides cares more about large structural forces that drive states.
He says Mao’s barrel-of-a-gun formula and Deng’s black-cat/white-cat formula are utilitarian and therefore inadequate for understanding politics and war.
Athens and Sparta did not need to fight from a utility perspective, but their opposed political personalities made war likely.
The United States attack on Iran is unintelligible if read only through utility, because Iran offered Trump a deal; the war has to be read through state personality and structural drive.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated diagnosis of the 2026 Iran war as of 2026-04-09.

claims.claim: Pericles funeral oration matters because speeches create common identity, hope, and coordinated action, revealing why a people fights a war.
Iran can resist the United States despite military inferiority because faith and symbolic action, not weapons alone, coordinate its people.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated diagnosis of Iran war conditions on 2026-04-09.

claims.claim: Jiang says Khamenei’s refusal to flee Tehran would turn his death into a speech: a home-defense sacrifice that gives Iranians energy, mourning, and faith.
Zhuge Liang and Cao Cao represent Chinese genius archetypes, but Thucydides treats such figures as secondary to structural forces.
The Peloponnesian War began because a dominant Sparta confronted a rising Athens, not because particular individuals disliked each other.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical analogy used in this lecture.

claims.claim: The better explanation for the Iran war is not Donald Trump personally but Anglo-American naval grand strategy to prevent a Eurasian Heartland power from controlling the World Island.
America attacks Iran, in Jiang’s model, to split the Heartland and prevent Russia and Iran from unifying Eurasia after Russia’s gains in Ukraine.
Jiang presents Dugin as the best contemporary geopolitical analyst because Dugin understands state psychology and structural positioning better than most analysts.
China faces major headwinds because the Iran war threatens global stability, resource continuity, and the export basis of China’s economy.
Jiang does not think Taiwan is the immediate flashpoint; Chinese overfishing in the South China Sea may bring China into conflict with neighbors who arrest Chinese fishermen.
The long-term regional conflict Jiang sees is water: Southeast Asian rivers flow from the Tibetan Plateau controlled by China, and resource wars have often been fought over water.
Ideas matter more than raw power because ideas coordinate and motivate populations into action.
Grand strategy is more important than geography because it is the organized response to geographic limits.
Without a theory of the world, a country becomes a pawn used by those who do have grand strategy.
Iran’s target selection is strategic: it should conserve limited weapons and pressure GCC states rather than waste attacks on Jerusalem or Tel Aviv.
Jiang says Iran wants to force America into a ground invasion because ground war is the only domain where Iran can destroy or defeat the United States.
Israel’s strategic plan, in Jiang’s telling, is to have America and Iran destroy each other so Israel becomes the dominant regional actor.
Secret societies are not omnipotent controllers; Jiang says they are groups trying to maintain power across political transformations by using accumulated historical knowledge.
In Jiang’s model, such groups cannot stop imperial decline but can accelerate events and position themselves to profit from it.
Jiang says the alleged Albert Pike letter about three world wars is fabricated, while Pike’s Morals and Dogma is real and worth reading.
He characterizes the relevant elite project as creating reason and order by reducing human agency, diversity, and conflict.
Artificial intelligence is presented as the mechanism for a surveillance/control grid whose economic logic is weak but control logic is strong.
Iran understands Gulf ruling elites as loyal to the Anglo-American empire and seeks to create internal conflict that overthrows them.
Jiang predicts that GCC royal families will not survive the Iran war.
In Jiang’s Illuminati account, Jacob Frank, Mayer Rothschild, and Adam Weishaupt are the three key figures because they combine messianic, financial, and Jesuit/organizational components.
Secret societies, in Jiang’s account, benefit from Middle East war by manipulating events and telling religious stories that mobilize followers.
Christian Zionist followers sincerely believe Middle East war can usher in the Messiah or return of Jesus, while powerful manipulators mainly care about maintaining power.
Jiang says closure or effective disruption of the Red Sea and Strait of Hormuz is likely because Houthis can strike shipping lanes and Saudi/GCC energy infrastructure.
Japan will run down oil reserves in roughly seven to eight months and therefore must act before the energy disruption fully matures.
Jiang expects Japan to remilitarize, join a coalition, and eventually send force to the Middle East, probably about a year away, with South Korea participating too.
claims.temporal_scope: Prediction made on 2026-04-09, roughly one-year horizon.

claims.claim: China and Japan have opposed military doctrines: China is primarily a land army focused on borders, while Japan is a strong aggressive naval power.
If Japan militarizes under cover of the Iran war, China has little ability to stop it because Japan’s strength is maritime.
For Japan, control of seas in the South China Sea is a matter of life and death; for China, Jiang says retreating behind borders is always available.
Japan wants to use the Middle East war as an excuse to remilitarize, but it can still buy oil from Russia and the United States, so it is not truly under immediate resource threat.
North Korea’s best grand-strategy move is to exploit distraction in Iran and its mutual defense pact with Russia by threatening South Korea and Japan for bribes rather than necessarily going to war.
Conflict between the Philippines and China would be part of a larger Southeast Asian alliance with Vietnam, Malaysia, Indonesia, and others against China.
Jiang says America is going to lose the Middle East war and thereby lose access to GCC petrodollar funding.

American grand strategy will shift from maintaining Pax Americana and dollar-finance globalization to retreating into the Western Hemisphere and selling resources, oil, rare earths, agriculture, and weapons to the rest of the world.
China and America can remain friendly in this model because the real conflict would be Japan-China and China-Southeast Asia, from which America profits as supplier.

China cannot replace America as world leader because historically it has not cared about conquering or organizing the world; Belt and Road is trade built on top of Pax Americana, not a grand strategy.
When global conflict blocks trade, Jiang expects China’s historical pattern to be retreating home, closing borders, and protecting its borders.
Jiang says it is pointless to design a grand strategy for China because China has no friends, does not care about the world, and its policymakers do not want a world theory.
In the next two years, Jiang expects the Middle East war to reach an equilibrium where it does not end, U.S. ground troops are committed, GCC economies are destroyed, and the conflict drags on.
Jiang predicts massive discontent and uprising in the United States, with much depending on 2028 because he expects Trump will not leave office voluntarily.
claims.temporal_scope: Forecast for the period through 2028, made on 2026-04-09.

claims.claim: American defeat in the Middle East does not mean peace; Jiang expects the empire to redirect force toward North America, including possible pressure or invasion against Cuba, Canada, or Mexico.
Jiang’s Greater Israel scenario is that, as America leaves the Middle East, Israel absorbs CENTCOM equipment, personnel, and apparatus, while GCC states are destroyed and Iran is crippled.
Jiang says America is already in civil war between Wall Street financial elites, dominant since Clinton and Obama, and Silicon Valley AI counter-elites represented by Trump.
He identifies two major U.S. bubbles: a roughly two-trillion-dollar private credit bubble and an AI bubble in which leading companies control AI without making money.
Whichever elite controls Washington directs bailout policy: the current system bails out finance, while Trump’s success would redirect bailouts toward AI/Silicon Valley.
Trump benefits from starting wars because wars weaken the military-industrial complex and financial sector, creating vulnerability to an AI counterrevolution.
Jiang expects the financial elite to strike back and a street-level conflict among militias and groups to emerge.
The Wall Street versus Silicon Valley lens, Jiang says, makes Venezuela, Iran, North America, and China more intelligible.

signature_moments.moment: Nation-states are treated less like calculators than like personalities with souls and emotional states.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they have a soul they have a psychology
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture’s first major reversal against purely utilitarian Chinese realism.

signature_moments.moment: Speech replaces stratagem as the Western technology of war because it discloses and coordinates a people.
signature_moments.source_phrase: speeches are a way of revealing the personality and the outlook of a nation
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It prepares the later Iran argument, where sacrifice itself becomes a speech.

signature_moments.moment: The cat’s color suddenly matters: utility alone cannot explain political behavior.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It actually does matter a lot if it is black or white
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Jiang turns Deng’s famous pragmatism into the wrong lens for geopolitics.

signature_moments.moment: A speech becomes the national medium that tells people who they are and where they are going.
signature_moments.source_phrase: who they are what they are doing here and where they are going
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the public read a compact distinction between tactics and civilizational motivation.

signature_moments.moment: The dead leader’s refusal to leave becomes a speech without words.
signature_moments.source_phrase: his action is a speech in itself
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang’s sharpest bridge from Thucydidean speech to Iranian resistance.

signature_moments.moment: Historical force becomes an ocean wave: the great swimmer still cannot beat the current.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You are a great swimmer but you are not going to beat an ocean wave
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses the anti-great-man argument into an image.

signature_moments.moment: Iran is attacked not as a local enemy but as the seam that could join Eurasia.
signature_moments.source_phrase: split the Heartland in half
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns the Middle East war into a Eurasian map problem.

signature_moments.moment: Without grand strategy, China is not a player but a pawn in someone else’s game.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You will just be a pawn in the game
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the lecture’s public-facing thesis in one sentence.

signature_moments.moment: Grand strategy is defined as the answer to geography, not a slogan layered on top of it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Grand strategy is response to limitations of geography
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It connects the lecture’s map examples to the political claim that ideas organize action.

signature_moments.moment: The most provocative turn: Israel and Iran are not simply enemies if both paths exhaust the American empire.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they are working together to destroy the American empire
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is a memorable Jiang reversal, though it should be presented carefully as his strategic reading.

signature_moments.moment: Secret societies become surfers of imperial change, not gods outside history.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they cannot stop things from happening
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It prevents the Q&A from flattening into conspiracy omnipotence.

signature_moments.moment: AI is not justified by profit but by obedience: the machine makes sense as a control grid.
signature_moments.source_phrase: from a control perspective it does make a lot of sense
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This links the secret-society question back to Jiang’s larger agency-versus-control model.

signature_moments.moment: The British-made Gulf order is declared terminal.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the GCC will not survive this war
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the Middle East section a concrete institutional prediction.

signature_moments.moment: The rulers do not need to believe the religious story; the followers do, and that is enough to move them.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you need to tell a story to your followers that motivates them into action
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It links religion to Jiang’s broader speech-and-coordination thesis.

signature_moments.moment: Japan’s oil clock becomes the trigger for remilitarization.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they will run out in about 7 to 8 months
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The Q&A turns Middle East straits into an East Asian military timetable.

signature_moments.moment: Japan is described as a naval power so capable that, in a ships-only war, Jiang says it could beat America.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if the United States and Japan were to go to war ... Japan would win
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shocks the listener into treating Japan, not Taiwan, as a serious maritime actor.

signature_moments.moment: For China the sea can be abandoned; for Japan it is life and death.
signature_moments.source_phrase: for Japan it is a matter of life and death
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This contrast explains why the same maritime crisis produces different incentives.

signature_moments.moment: North Korea does not need war; menace itself can be monetized.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they will bribe me not to do anything
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the East Asia section a crisp coercion model.

signature_moments.moment: America’s retreat becomes a resource business model: let Asia fight, then sell both sides fuel and weapons.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I can sell oil I can sell resources and I can sell weapons
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang’s sharpest transition from Pax Americana to resource empire.

signature_moments.moment: China’s default global-conflict move is not world leadership but turtling up.
signature_moments.source_phrase: turtle up Retreat back into its home
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It answers the G2 question with a civilizational habit rather than a GDP forecast.

signature_moments.moment: China cannot be given a grand strategy because Jiang says the political desire for one is absent.
signature_moments.source_phrase: What is the point of developing a grand strategy
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sharpens the China section: the problem is not missing advice but missing appetite.

signature_moments.moment: A losing empire does not stop; it changes theaters.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if it is losing in Iran it is going to shift attention
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This connects Middle East defeat to North American coercion in Jiang’s model.

signature_moments.moment: Greater Israel appears as the inheritor of abandoned U.S. military machinery.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Israel absorbs all CENTCOM
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the answer’s most concrete image of imperial succession.

signature_moments.moment: America’s civil war is not red versus blue but Wall Street versus Silicon Valley.
signature_moments.source_phrase: civil war going on in America between Wall Street and Silicon Valley
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the final answer a compact internal-power map.

signature_moments.moment: War becomes a tool for weakening the old elite enough for the AI counter-elite to seize the state.
signature_moments.source_phrase: weakening the financial sector that makes them vulnerable to a counter revolution
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It ties foreign war to domestic elite replacement.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A Western geopolitical lens in which state personality, speech, fear, and structural forces matter more than clever tactics.
A Chinese tactical trick or scheme that Jiang says works best inside a shared culture but weakens across civilizational boundaries.
Jiang’s contrast between material-interest reasoning and state personality as a driver of conflict.
The Athenian public speech genre Jiang uses as a model of how war meaning and collective identity are produced.
Mackinder’s model, as Jiang presents it, that whoever controls the Eurasian pivot can control the World Island and world trade.
Dugin’s Russian grand-strategy frame in which Moscow offers an Orthodox, collectivist, spiritual alternative to decadent Faustian societies.
Dugin/Jiang term for Western societies driven by individualism, materialism, and expansion, later consumed by decadence and greed.
A theory of the world that lets a state respond to geographic limits and avoid being used by stronger strategists.
Jiang’s long-term warning that China’s control of upstream rivers may become a Southeast Asian conflict source.
A proposed deceptive attack scenario in the host’s question about Jerusalem and religious escalation.
Gulf Cooperation Council states that Jiang says Iran would pressure to draw America into ground war.
A control grid Jiang says can be installed after chaos to make people happy, obedient, and legible.
Albert Pike’s real Freemasonic text, contrasted with the fabricated three-world-wars letter.
The sincere religious follower base Jiang says is motivated by stories of Messiah or Jesus returning through Middle East war.
One of the energy chokepoints whose disruption the host and Jiang connect to East Asian energy security.
The naval strategic posture Jiang attributes to Japan, contrasted with China’s land-border focus.
The U.S.-led globalization system in which trade clears through dollar finance and Belt and Road can function.
GCC oil revenue returning to the American economy, which Jiang says funds the finance-centered U.S. model.
Jiang’s scenario in which Israel emerges as the dominant Middle East power by absorbing U.S. CENTCOM apparatus after American defeat.
The North American sphere Jiang says U.S. officials treat as subject to American coercive control.
The Silicon Valley and AI-aligned actors Jiang says Trump represents against Wall Street financial elites.
A roughly two-trillion-dollar bubble Jiang identifies in private lending among privileged companies.
Jiang’s term for leading AI companies controlling the field while not making money, with bailout stakes attached.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates the Peloponnesian War to roughly 431-404 BCE and uses it as the origin case for Western geopolitical reasoning.
Jiang dates Dugin’s Foundations of Geopolitics to 1996 and uses it as background for Putin-era Russian strategy.
On 2026-04-09 Jiang predicts Japanese remilitarization and Middle East naval deployment probably within about a year.
Jiang explicitly points to 2028, when Trump is supposed to leave office, as a likely domestic crisis point.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript says Iran’s supreme leader was killed; this is Jiang’s scenario claim in the source and is preserved as source-grounded content, not independently verified fact.
The host’s question includes ASR-damaged “false threat,” almost certainly false flag from context.
These ASR segments are badly damaged; claims are kept broad and tied only to the clear strategic movement.
Jiang uses loaded terms around Gulf rulers, crypto Jews, Frankists, Jesuits, and Illuminati; preserve attribution and avoid presenting these as external facts.
